artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Just wrapped up another round of edits on the council memo and I’m staring at this title again: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet.” Every time I type it, my brain wants to tack on all the messy reasons behind it – the December 18, 2023 accident with Mark Ellison on Highway 210 by Brainerd High, the January 9, 2024 collision by St. Joseph’s with that suspected DUI driver that totaled the other unit, the near‑miss with Lauren’s van on East River Road on January 12. None of that belongs in the public title, but it’s what’s rattling around in my head when people say it’s “just” a truck.

I keep thinking about how different this winter could have been if Truck #17 hadn’t been down during the February 23, 2024 storm when Highland Scenic iced over and we ended up with that March 4 notice‑of‑claim letter from David Carlson. Add in the 60–70 hour weeks since December and Dr. Olson at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic telling me on January 8 that my blood pressure is in the danger zone, and it’s hard not to take this personally.

Meanwhile, normal life goes on: Lauren’s choir concert Thursday at Forestview, Tyler’s late ice at Essentia Health Sports Center tomorrow (8:45–10:15 p.m., ugh), and your parents coming over Sunday to see the progress on the basement. I still think if we can get this truck approved on February 3 and ordered before lead times get worse, maybe next winter I’m not dragging myself from a 14‑hour shift straight to the AmericInn in Baxter just so I don’t fall asleep on Highway 25 again.

I’ll be home by 6:30 if the phones stay quiet. Can you have Tyler start the chili?

– M

metadata.subject: Why this “one truck” feels like a lot more

artifacts.content:
Sarah,

Finally catching my breath long enough to write back. Dad’s situation has been weighing on me more than I’ve admitted. Since his early‑stage dementia diagnosis at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic last June and all those visits with Dr. Amanda Schultz, I’ve been quietly covering around $450 a month for his counseling and meds. On top of that, after you got laid off from the Baxter Target in October 2024 and you and Jason slipped two months behind on the $1,150 mortgage, I’ve been sending $300–$400 a month so you don’t lose the house. Add the $9,200 we still owe on Tyler’s ACL surgery at St. Joe’s from March 2023, and you can probably see why I’m so dug in about not pushing for a levy bump to pay for this truck.

Finance wants to know why I’m insisting we use the Equipment Replacement Reserve and keep the $298,750 for the tandem within the 2026 CIP instead of asking for more tax capacity. I keep picturing Mom and Dad on fixed income, and neighbors like Jason on Maple Ridge trying to juggle special assessments and hardship plans. After the State Auditor attorney emailed me in August 2023 about that 2022 blade we bought from cousin Mark’s St. Cloud shop, and the September 14 meeting at City Hall with the Finance Director and City Attorney, I’m hypersensitive about anything that even looks like I’m playing games with money.

On the home front, Lauren’s braces bill from Lakes Area Dental is still hanging over us, and Tyler’s already asking about summer hockey camps. We’ll make it work, but it means I stare a little harder at every dollar I move around at work.

We’re still on for coffee with Dad Sunday at Good Sam?

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Money, Dad, and this truck mess

artifacts.content:
Personal work journal – 2025-02-06

I need to get my own head straight about why I’m pushing so hard on the Truck #17 replacement before next winter.

1. Safety/near‑misses
- January 12, 2024 on East River Road: brakes fading while I was plowing and nearly sliding into the minivan with Lauren and three other Forestview kids. Having to explain that to Officer Mark Jensen at the scene is still burned into my brain.
- February 23, 2024 Highland Scenic storm: #17 down in the Brainerd garage, route uncovered, then the March 4 notice‑of‑claim letter from David Carlson about the back injury. That’s exactly the kind of thing the League of Minnesota Cities risk consultant warned me about in April 2023 after the February 9, 2023 rear‑end at Washington & 6th (Tom Larson driving, Honda Civic, $6,800 total).

2. Staff impacts
- Dave Carlson’s back/PT at Brainerd Lakes Orthopaedic since January 2024 – if we don’t replace the truck, I’m basically committing him to more 10–12 hour shifts.
- Jim Larson’s February 5, 2024 safety complaint after the January 29–30 storm and the steering issues coming back into the Thiesse Road yard. Regardless of who remembers what, the root problem is the equipment.

3. Personal health/family
- Dr. Karen Olson’s hypertension warning on January 8, 2024.
- Dad’s wandering at Good Samaritan – Bethany during the December 27–28, 2023 blizzard while I was out covering a route because #17 died.

Bottom line: this isn’t about a shiny new toy; it’s about not rolling the dice on the same truck for another winter.

Event: Council P&F Committee – Snowplow Replacement Discussion
Date: 2026-02-03
Time: 6:00–6:45 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Council, City Administrator, Finance Director, Michael Johnson (PW Director)

Notes for myself:

• Item title on agenda: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Public Works Fleet.” Keep it clean – no reference to the December 18, 2023 Mark Ellison accident on Hwy 210 near Brainerd High or the January 9, 2024 DUI collision by St. Joseph’s Medical Center, even though both are part of the backstory.

• Be ready if anyone asks “why now” when the truck is in the 2026 CIP:
  – Mention repeated breakdowns of Truck #17, including being down during the February 23, 2024 storm on Highland Scenic Road (tie to service reliability, not the March 4 notice‑of‑claim letter).
  – Emphasize 10–12 month lead times; need truck in service before 2027–2028 winter.
  – Talk about operator ergonomics and safety – Dave Carlson’s back issues and PT at Brainerd Lakes Orthopaedic since January 2024.

• Avoid personal stuff in public comments:
  – No mention of my 60–70 hour weeks or Dr. Olson’s hypertension warning.
  – Don’t bring up Dad’s dementia, Good Sam calls during the December 27–28, 2023 blizzard, or Tyler helping in the PW yard during the February 7–9, 2025 storm.

Bring: updated CPV quote from Northland Truck & Equipment, funding breakdown (Capital Equipment Fund + Equipment Replacement Reserve + 2025 surplus), and map of northeast quadrant routes (Harrison/Maple/Oak).

To: City HR Director
From: michael.johnson@ci.brainerd.mn.us
Date: 2025-02-07
Subject: Follow-up on Truck #17 concerns and staffing

Karen,

I wanted to put a few things in writing after Jim Larson’s February 5 safety complaint about being asked to take Truck #17 back out during the January 29–30 storm after he reported steering issues coming into the Thiesse Road yard.

Whatever the differing recollections about that night, I agree with Jim on the core point: #17 is at the end of its life. We’ve already seen what happens when we’re short a truck – e.g., the February 23, 2024 Highland Scenic storm when #17 was down and we later got the March 4 notice‑of‑claim letter from David Carlson about the back injury. Before that, there was the February 9, 2023 rear‑end at Washington & 6th (Tom Larson driving, Honda Civic, $6,800 total) that the League of Minnesota Cities risk consultant flagged in our April 2023 call as the kind of pattern they worry about with aging equipment.

On the staffing side, I’m also worried about the toll on people. Dave Carlson has been in PT at Brainerd Lakes Orthopaedic Clinic since January 2024 for back issues, but he keeps volunteering for 10–12 hour shifts because he doesn’t want to leave us hanging. I’m not comfortable banking our snow response on one guy pushing through pain.

For my part, Dr. Karen Olson at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic told me at my January 8, 2024 appointment that my 60–70 hour weeks and double overnights when #17 fails are driving my blood pressure into dangerous territory. I’m trying to keep that separate from work decisions, but it’s hard to ignore.

I’m moving the replacement request forward with Council and will keep you looped in.

Michael

metadata.subject: Follow-up on Truck #17 concerns and staffing

artifacts.content:
Home finance / family planning note – 2025-03-06

Trying to see the bigger picture with this truck, work, and our own money.

Work side:
- Truck #17 replacement cost: $298,750 (CPV chassis + plow/wing/underbody + stainless sander + delivery/tax).
- Mark Stevens already dinged me in my February 2024 review over the $9,200 in unplanned repairs at Nuss Truck & Equipment since July 2023 and warned a second year could affect my 2026 merit increase.
- After the August 2023 email from the State Auditor’s attorney about the 2022 blade purchase from cousin Mark’s St. Cloud shop, and the September 14, 2023 meeting with Finance and City Attorney Karen Olson, I really don’t want any more questions about “why didn’t you replace this sooner.”

Home side:
- If my 2026 merit bump gets cut, Emily and I probably have to pause Tyler’s $425/month hockey at Essentia Health Sports Center and scale back extra principal on the Maple Ridge mortgage.
- We already pulled from my 401(a) supplemental account (opened with the City in 2015) once; I don’t want to yank another $8,000 for the basement apartment we’re trying to finish for Mom and Dad by October 2025 because they can’t afford Edgewood Brainerd.
- Still owe $9,200 on Tyler’s ACL surgery at St. Joseph’s (March 2023), plus Lauren’s braces at Lakes Area Dental.
- Since January I’ve put about $1,200 on the card for AmericInn in Baxter stays after 14‑hour plow shifts, ever since I nodded off for a second on Highway 25 on January 18, 2024.

Conclusion: getting the truck replaced on schedule isn’t just a “nice to have” – it’s tied directly to my workload, health, and whether we can keep our own family budget on track.

Em,

Quick debrief before I forget the details from this crazy stretch.

During the February 7–9, 2025 blizzard we only had three functioning plows, including that 2009 tandem that kept stalling on South 6th Street. I did something I’m not proud of and dragged Tyler into the Public Works yard on Maple Ridge Drive at 4:30 a.m. to help shovel and throw sand by hand. Laura Peterson called me into her office at City Hall on February 12 and gave me a verbal warning for having an underage, non‑employee in the yard during an active storm. She wasn’t wrong – she flat‑out said that if anything had happened to him, both the city and I could’ve been in serious trouble.

It all ties back to the same thing: not enough reliable equipment and too many hours. Ever since December 2023 when #17 started dying every other storm, I’ve been stacking 60–70 hour weeks and double overnights. Dr. Olson at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic told me at that January 8, 2024 appointment that my blood pressure is in the “elevated risk for a cardiac event” zone if I keep this up. Between that and the night in January when I had to stay at the AmericInn in Baxter again because I was nodding off on Highway 25, I’m realizing I’m not 30 anymore.

I’m also still thinking about Dad during the December 27–28, 2023 blizzard, when Good Samaritan – Bethany called three times overnight about him trying to leave the building while I was out covering a route because #17 died. That’s the night I decided we can’t just “limp along” with this truck.

I know you’d rather I disconnect and just watch a movie with the kids, and I’m trying. Getting this replacement through is part of that.

I’ll be home after the 6 p.m. budget check‑in.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Why I’m pushing so hard on this truck (and Tyler in the yard)
